summary: Gruber describes his experience optimizing NetNewsWire for iPhone by radically paring down his feed subscriptions. Despite subscribing to 155 feeds on his Mac, he found the iPhone version increasingly unusable with all that data. Rather than selectively disabling a few noisy feeds, he turned them all off and re-enabled only about 20 high-signal feeds. The result was a faster, more enjoyable mobile reading experience. He concludes that iPhone apps need not just optimized interfaces but appropriately scaled data.
key_points: NewsGator's location feature allows different feed lists for Mac and iPhone, but selectively pruning a few feeds wasn't enough
The subtractive approach (start from zero, add back only essentials) worked far better than the reductive approach (start from everything, remove some)
Mobile computing demands fundamentally different data volumes, not just different UI
The 20 best feeds out of 155 created a better iPhone experience — faster launches, less network usage, higher quality content
money_quotes: What I want in an iPhone feed reader isn't just a little bit different than what I want in a Mac feed reader, it's a lot different.

Rather than going through my full list of feeds and turning some off, I turned them all off, then went through and re-enabled about 20 feeds — the ones I like best, with the highest signal-to-noise ratios, and which would be most enjoyable in those I'm bored, give me something to read moments.
It's not enough for an iPhone app to sport an iPhone-optimized user interface. It needs iPhone-sized data, too.
The result is that NetNewsWire on my iPhone launches faster, loads less data over the network, and pretty much only shows me stuff I really might want to read.
key_insight: Mobile apps need more than a mobile-optimized interface — they need mobile-scaled data, because the context of use is fundamentally different from desktop.
